message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>Regular exercise is known to boost immunity, increase immune response to fight infection, as well as speeding up recovery times and healing processes. This study seeks to assess if exercising regularly pre-SARS-CoV-2 (COVID-19) and/or BMI status has an effect on recovery time.</jats:p>
                <jats:p>A total of 215 people infected with COVD-19 from the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia took part in this study (age 36±16 years, mass 72±15 kg, stature 166±11 cm). Only 10 patients were physically active and fulfil WHO physical activity requirement (Age 30±7 years, Mass 77±9 kg, Stature 176±1 cm).</jats:p>
                <jats:p>There was a significant difference in recovery time between active and inactive patients (P = 0.00) with active patients’ recovery 2.7 times faster than inactive patients. Active patients showed a lower BMI level (p = 0.043).</jats:p>
                <jats:p>Anthropometric measurement characteristics and the fitness level could be used in decision making scenarios for the estimation of the risk of complications in patients with COVID-19.</jats:p>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Novelty:</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Covid-19 physical active patients shows faster recovery time.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>
                        Active patients recorded a BMI of over 25kg/m
                        <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                        , recovered faster than those inactive patients with similar BMI’s.
                      </jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: The effect of BMI and physical activity levels on the duration of symptomatic days with Covid-19 infection

reference.article-title: A Review of Prevalence of Obesity in Saudi Arabia
Effects of resistance training on resting immune parameters in women

reference.unstructured: Ministry of health. 2020. COVID-19 cases with the mortality rate. https://www.moh.gov.sa/en/Pages/Default.aspx

reference.article-title: Prevalence of hospital PCR-confirmed COVID-19 cases in patients with chronic inflammatory and autoimmune rheumatic diseases
